content:
4 min read
Just now

--

“There are only two hard things in Computer Science: cache invalidation and naming things.”
— Phil Karlton (popularized by Martin Fowler)
Despite being decades old, this quote still resonates with every software engineer.
At first glance, it sounds like a joke. After all, how hard can naming a variable or a function be?
But spend enough time working on production systems, and you’ll realize that naming isn’t about choosing words — it’s about communicating intent.
The compiler doesn’t care what you call something.
Humans do.
And humans spend far more time reading code than writing it.
A name is the first piece of documentation
Imagine you come across this code:
double amount = calculate(order);Can you confidently answer these questions?

• Is amount the subtotal?

• Does it include tax?

• Has the discount already been applied?

• Is it in USD or INR?

• Is it the amount to charge or the amount to refund?
Without opening calculate(), you have no idea.
Now compare it with:
double finalPayableAmount = calculateFinalPayableAmount(order);Suddenly, the code becomes self-explanatory.
The implementation may be identical.
The readability is not.
A good name saves the next engineer from opening five more files just to understand one line of code.
Bad names create hidden technical debt
Most engineers think technical debt comes from poor architecture.
In reality, poor naming creates technical debt too.
Consider this:
process(data);What is data?
Customer data?
Payment data?
Analytics data?
Configuration data?
Or this:
handle(response);Which response?
HTTP response?
Database response?
Payment gateway response?
AI model response?
Names like data, temp, obj, result, info, and value don't communicate intent.
Instead, they force every reader to reconstruct the author’s thought process mentally.
Now imagine a codebase with thousands of variables named this way.
Nothing is technically broken.
Yet everything feels difficult to understand.
Every bad name increases cognitive load
Software engineering is already complex.
Developers are constantly thinking about:

• Business requirements

• Concurrency

• Performance

• Edge cases

• Security

• Scalability
The last thing they should spend time on is figuring out what x, obj2, or tempData actually represent.
Every unclear name adds a tiny mental tax.
One unclear variable isn’t a problem.
Hundreds of them are.
This is why experienced engineers obsess over naming.
Not because they’re perfectionists.
Because reducing cognitive load is one of the easiest ways to improve maintainability.

Naming is really about modeling the business
The hardest part isn’t finding a better English word.
The hardest part is understanding the domain.
Imagine you’re building an e-commerce platform.
Should this entity be called:

• Customer

• Buyer

• Shopper

• User

• Account Holder

• Subscriber
All are valid.
Only one accurately reflects your business.
Now imagine hundreds of developers using different words for the same concept.
Eventually, your codebase starts speaking multiple languages.
One service calls it a “Customer.”
Another calls it a “Buyer.”
A third uses “User.”
The confusion isn’t caused by code.
It’s caused by inconsistent vocabulary.
This is why Domain-Driven Design emphasizes creating a Ubiquitous Language — a shared vocabulary used consistently across code, documentation, and conversations.
Good software starts with good language.

The problem gets worse over time
When a project begins, naming is easy.
You have three classes.
Ten methods.
A few variables.
Fast forward three years.
The codebase has:

• Hundreds of microservices

• Thousands of classes

• Millions of lines of code

• Dozens of teams contributing simultaneously
Now naming isn’t just about clarity.
It’s about consistency.
One poorly named abstraction can spread across an entire organization.
Renaming it later becomes expensive because APIs, documentation, dashboards, and dependent services all rely on it.
Names are surprisingly permanent.

This is where AI has become incredibly useful
One of the most underrated benefits of AI coding assistants is naming.
Consider this code:
List<Order> list = repository.findOrdersByStatus(PENDING);Most developers would eventually rename list to something meaningful.
AI does it instantly:
List<Order> pendingOrders = repository.findOrdersByStatus(PENDING);It understands:

• Method names

• Types

• Surrounding code

• Coding conventions

• Patterns learned from millions of repositories
Instead of spending mental energy deciding between orders, list, pendingOrders, or filteredOrders, developers get a strong suggestion immediately.
It removes friction from one of the most repetitive parts of programming.

But AI hasn’t solved naming
Here’s a more interesting example:
Customer customer;Should it actually be:

• buyer

• merchant

• subscriber

• accountHolder

• client

• partner
AI can suggest all of them.
Only humans know which one is correct.
Because this decision isn’t based on syntax.
It’s based on product requirements, business rules, and shared domain language.
AI understands code remarkably well.
Engineers understand why the business exists.
That difference still matters.

Great names make code disappear
The best codebases aren’t necessarily the ones with the cleverest algorithms.
They’re the ones where reading the code feels effortless.
When names accurately describe intent, the implementation almost reads like English.
Developers spend less time decoding and more time solving problems.
And that’s ultimately the goal of clean code.
Not fewer lines.
Not shorter methods.
Not more design patterns.
Less thinking about the code itself.

Final thoughts
AI has transformed software development.
It writes boilerplate, generates tests, explains legacy code, and even suggests meaningful variable names.
But naming is more than autocomplete.
It’s about choosing the language your team will use to think about the problem.
The compiler only needs code that runs.
Your teammates need code they can understand.
And that’s why, even in the age of AI, naming things remains one of the hardest — and most important — problems in software engineering.
